competitors.name: Databricks
competitors.curator: Rebuilt from the internal Klue export (curated by Dan Soceanu, with Phil Weiss and Jennifer Evans) merged with 2026 public research.

changelog.note: Replaced stale internal financial figures ($62B valuation / $1.4B revenue / ~8,800 employees, both dated 2024) with the full funding and revenue trajectory through June 2026: valuation $43B->$62B->$100B+->$134B->reportedly in talks at $165-175B; revenue run-rate $4B->$4.8B->$5.4B (Databricks-confirmed) with a $6.9B analyst estimate (Sacra) for June 2026. Sourced from Databricks newsroom press releases plus The Information/Sacra reporting.

Rebuilt to match the retired Klue battlecard's full structure. Merged internal export content (background, M&A, product taxonomy, Their View/SAS View, discovery questions, Gartner history, Trustworthy AI messaging) with current 2026 research (DAIS 2026 announcements, G2/Gartner Peer Insights/BARC review data).

Moved dashboard from a single self-contained HTML file to a static site (index.html) driven by this external data.json, for Vercel deployment. Removed the live-editable Insights from the Field / Recent Wins boxes, since they relied on Claude-artifact-only storage that doesn't exist on a plain static host.
Initial lightweight card built from public research only (Data + AI Summit 2026 announcements, review sites).

sections.background: Founded in 2013 in San Francisco by seven researchers out of UC Berkeley's AMPlab - including former CEOs Ali Ghodsi and Ion Stoica - Databricks grew directly out of the Spark query-processing engine work that began at Berkeley in 2009. It open-sourced its MLflow model-management framework in 2018. Valuation trajectory: $43B (2023) -> $62B after its Series J closed December 17, 2024 -> $100B+ after a $1B Series K in September 2025 -> $134B after a Series L of $4B+ that closed December 2025 and was topped up in February 2026 to over $7B in total financing ($5B equity plus $2B debt capacity; investors include JPMorganChase, Microsoft, Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley and Qatar Investment Authority) -> as of June 2026, reportedly in talks (per The Information) for a fresh round at $165-175B, alongside bankers increasingly expecting an IPO by late 2026, though CEO Ali Ghodsi has said an offering is 'not top of mind' immediately after the Series L. Revenue trajectory: Databricks officially reported a $4B run-rate in September 2025 (+50% YoY), $4.8B in December 2025 (+55% YoY), and $5.4B in February 2026 (+65% YoY, AI products alone crossing $1.4B run-rate, data warehousing crossing $1.5B, net revenue retention above 140%, 800+ customers spending $1M+ annually). Third-party tracking (Sacra) estimates the run-rate reached roughly $6.9B by June 2026, growing near 80% YoY, though this figure is an analyst estimate rather than a Databricks-confirmed number.
sections.marketStrategy: Databricks markets itself hard as a single, complete end-to-end data-and-analytics platform under the banner of 'unified data analytics,' adding value through integrated add-ons rather than a fundamentally new category each time. Its positioning has shifted five times over the company's history - each shift claims the same unification territory under a new name:
positioningEvolution: Proprietary Spark distribution
Database management vendor
Analytics platform
Data lakehouse provider
Data Intelligence platform for AI model building and governance (current)
sections.verticals: Databricks sells into essentially every major vertical: energy and utilities, federal government, financial services, gaming, healthcare, life sciences, manufacturing, retail, and more.
sections.partners: Primary cloud partners are Microsoft Azure, AWS and Google Cloud Platform, with notable go-to-market relationships alongside Tableau, Avanade and Cognizant. Databricks also runs an open data/AI marketplace so third parties can list models, notebooks, apps and solutions directly into customer workspaces - aimed at reducing proprietary lock-in friction (at least on paper).

top.point: Spark underpins everything Databricks does.
subs: Scala is difficult to support and program; Python is generally needed to supplement Spark processing.
Spark was retrofitted to run iterative analytics using DAG files and RDDs.
Like SAS's CAS engine, Spark requires its own copies of data to process.

top.point: Databricks has been 100% serverless since July 2024.
subs: The platform was re-engineered for pay-as-you-go processing with automated tuning and maintenance of compute resources - easier to start, but reviewers still flag cost unpredictability at scale (see Strengths & Weaknesses).

top.point: Growth is increasingly acquisition-fueled.
subs: $1.3B for MosaicML (2023), $1B for Tabular (2024), plus Lilac AI, Einblick and Arcion - expect continued tuck-ins funded by the 2026 raise.

top.point: Databricks does not run on-premises.
subs: Cloud-only (AWS, Azure, GCP) limits options for hybrid, air-gapped or mainframe-dependent customers.

top.point: Despite 'open' positioning, the platform is becoming more proprietary.
subs: Unity Catalog now underpins nearly everything a user does with data on the platform - a real, if unspoken, lock-in vector.

top.point: The competitive field has widened past a simple Databricks-vs-Snowflake framing.
subs: Snowflake remains the most-cited alternative on price (~30% cheaper) and ease of use, but Databricks itself now frames 2026 platform decisions as a four-way race against Snowflake, BigQuery and Microsoft Fabric.

offerings.overview: Databricks is a highly scalable platform, deployable in-cloud only, built around an interactive notebook/coding environment, monitoring tools and security controls. Despite marketing aimed at broadening the audience (AI/BI, Genie), the core experience is still designed for coders and developers with specialized skills.

items.name: AI/BI
items.desc: Dashboard-building and NLU layer, built on Einblick technology (acquired Jan 2024).

items.name: Mosaic AI / DBRX
items.desc: Build LLMs from scratch; high control over models, RAG support, open-source vector search, feature engineering.

items.name: Data Science Workspace (DSW)
items.desc: Shared notebook environment for TensorFlow, Keras, scikit-learn and other frameworks.

items.name: Managed MLflow
items.desc: Open-source model deployment, experiment tracking and metadata management.

items.name: Unified Data Service (UDS)
items.desc: Databricks Runtime + Delta Lake (Parquet-format storage) + Delta Engine query acceleration.

items.name: Enterprise Cloud Service (ECS)
items.desc: Auto-configures cloud infrastructure, removing traditional setup complexity.

items.name: Redash
items.desc: Drag-and-drop visualization front end for casual users consuming lakehouse data.

items.name: CustomerLake
items.desc: Agentic Customer Data Platform launched Jun 2026 - identity resolution, segmentation and activation natively in Databricks.

items.name: Genie One / Genie Ontology
items.desc: "Agentic co-worker" for business teams plus a live, self-updating business-context layer.

items.name: Agent Bricks
items.desc: Developer platform to build, evaluate and govern production AI agents on governed data.

items.name: Lakeflow / Lakebase
items.desc: No-code-to-code ETL pipelines; unified transactional database layer (GA Jan 2026).

items.name: Unity AI Gateway
items.desc: Governance layer across agents, models and MCP tools, both on and off Databricks.

theirView: Positions itself as a unified data analytics platform spanning data science, engineering and business, claiming to be the 'first unified data analytics engine' in the market.
Claims the platform removes the complexity of data science at scale, integrating with popular ML frameworks and deploying models from a single environment.
With Genie One, claims to move beyond copilots into 'agentic co-workers' that act directly on enterprise data, documents and applications.
sasView: The open-source framework is fundamentally code-centric and labor-intensive; even the newer AI/BI dashboard layer targets technical users first, not business analysts.
Org-wide deployment is hard in practice - coding skills are scarce and easy to silo by team.
Pulling from one central repository reduces duplication somewhat, but every user and every open-source tool bolted back on tends to recreate silos over time.
Automation across the full analytics lifecycle - data quality, visual exploration, decisioning - is thin. Databricks covers engineering and modeling well, not the decision step.
Every data request routes through the proprietary Unity Catalog, which becomes its own complexity layer despite the 'open' positioning.

theirView: Complete SaaS cloud experience, easily provisioned, with access to the broader Databricks ecosystem.
Collaborative workspace tied to Unity Catalog metadata.
'Photon' query engine claimed faster than scikit-learn and Spark.
Built-in AI-assistant integration.
sasView: Databricks notebooks only work inside a Databricks environment - customers are buying into the whole platform to get value.
Spark can't handle every type of data processing, and Python execution is mostly single-threaded.
SAS Viya Workbench auto-provisions Python kernels for blended-language work, giving Python users a scikit-learn-inspired API backed by SAS-grade performance.
Viya Workbench is easy to customize per team and scales compute on demand to match workload, reducing bottlenecks.

strengths: Engineering-grade scale and performance on large, complex data and ML workloads (Spark/Photon).
Deep open-source credibility (Spark, Delta Lake, MLflow) that resonates strongly with technical buyers.
Very fast product cadence and a well-capitalized growth story that impresses executives.
Genuinely multi-cloud, reducing hyperscaler lock-in concerns.
weaknesses: Steep learning curve - reviewers repeatedly cite complexity and a business-user support gap (BARC key-user-support score: 5.9/10).
Compute cost is hard to predict; multiple reviews describe costs 'growing at an astronomical rate' without close monitoring.
Governance has historically been fragmented across workspaces; Unity Catalog is improving this but still needs dedicated platform engineering effort.
Native BI/dashboarding is less mature than category leaders and often needs a third-party tool bolted on.

questions.q: Do you have bottlenecks that prevent certain projects from being undertaken or completed?
questions.a: SAS raises the analytics maturity of every user, not just coders - GUI-based data access/staging that removes the need for coding, responsible-AI best practices baked into model development, a full breadth of packaged analytics (forecasting, optimization, simulation, AML, network analysis and more), and a single persisted copy of data shared across applications and users.

questions.q: What kinds of difficulties do you have getting projects into production?
questions.a: Both platforms see friction here - pivot on who can actually do the work. Databricks is built mostly for highly skilled coders; SAS supports every persona. Databricks can underperform on smaller, less-parallelizable data due to architectural overhead, and model management/monitoring is a known gap SAS automates.

questions.q: Do you have an on-premises data center, or do people need to work fully offline?
questions.a: Databricks is cloud-only - pay-as-you-go on AWS, Azure or GCP, with no on-premises or mainframe option. That's a hard blocker for regulated or air-gapped environments.

questions.q: How important is it for you to incorporate ethical considerations into your AI development?
questions.a: SAS has a formal Data Ethics practice advising both internal development and customers, with trustworthy-AI guidance built into every phase of the model lifecycle. Lean on this whenever governance or responsible AI comes up.

analystNote.text: Per the most recent internal tracking, both SAS and Databricks were named Leaders in the Gartner Magic Quadrant for Data Science and Machine Learning Platforms (2024) - SAS has held Leader status for 9 consecutive years vs. Databricks' 4, with Databricks scoring lower on 'completeness of vision.' Confirm this is still accurate against the current Magic Quadrant before using it in a live deal.

attack: Ask what their fully-loaded compute cost was last quarter versus budget - DBU-based pricing plus compute is a well-documented sore spot.
Ask who on their team can actually build and maintain Spark/ML pipelines today - Databricks assumes a technical bench many analytics teams don't have in-house.
Question Databricks' inability to support low-code/no-code, and its speed to usable results without a coding team.
Probe how they'd explain a model's decision to an examiner or regulator today - a real gap between an engineering platform and a governed decisioning platform.
defend: 'SAS is legacy and on-prem' - Viya is fully cloud-native, and the Viya (CAS) massively parallel engine shares persisted data in memory across users and applications, something Databricks doesn't do.

'SAS is a closed black box' - Viya supports open languages (Python, R) alongside SAS code, publishes models directly into Databricks Spark, and integrates with Snowflake and Microsoft Fabric - additive, not either/or.

On GenAI specifically, SAS leads on NLP, synthetic data generation, copilot automation and ModelOps. Decisioning is a category where SAS holds an advantage over nearly every vendor, Databricks included.

sections.reframe: Don't compete on raw model performance and scalability for individual workloads - that's Databricks' strongest ground. Redirect the prospect to scaling analytics across the whole organization: a code-first platform creates a bottleneck around scarce coding talent, while SAS raises the analytics maturity of both 'clickers' and coders at once, improving productivity and profitability org-wide. Databricks will also push customers to standardize their analytics lifecycle on its platform - let that message work in SAS's favor by highlighting easier deployment and automatic model monitoring that Databricks lacks.
sections.betterTogether: Some accounts are deeply invested in Databricks or strong proponents of it. In those cases, don't force a rip-and-replace - run a complementary sales motion positioning SAS primarily around data management, governance and decisioning on top of the customer's existing Databricks investment.

trustworthyAI.definition: Trustworthy AI: systems designed, developed and deployed with human-centricity in mind - fairness, transparency, robustness, and safeguards to promote human agency and prevent harm.
trustworthyAI.theirs: Databricks states that data security is a priority, with client privacy close behind, backed by a Security & Trust Center and a public 'Commitment to Responsible AI' blog post - but it doesn't appear to be operationalized into a formal cross-lifecycle practice the way SAS's is.
trustworthyAI.ours: SAS's approach embeds fairness and oversight at every step of the AI and analytics journey: robust data governance, transparent AI, and secure ModelOps processes.

competitors.name: Microsoft
competitors.curator: Built from public research (Microsoft Fabric documentation, G2/Gartner/BARC reviews, SAS-Microsoft partnership announcements). No internal Klue export available yet - Product Claims rebuttal, discovery questions and analyst-recognition fields are flagged needs input.

changelog.note: Initial card built from public research.

sections.background: Microsoft Fabric launched in May 2023, consolidating Azure Synapse Analytics, Azure Data Factory, Power BI and Azure Data Lake Storage into a single SaaS analytics platform built on OneLake. Microsoft is simultaneously one of SAS's most important go-to-market partners - SAS is a Gold sponsor at Microsoft's own FABCON conference, and SAS Decision Builder runs natively on Fabric via the Microsoft Marketplace - and a growing analytics competitor through Fabric and Power BI.
sections.marketStrategy: Microsoft's strategy is bundling: Fabric is priced and positioned to be simpler and cheaper than assembling Synapse, Data Factory and Power BI Premium separately, and leans hard on existing Microsoft 365/Azure/Entra ID relationships. Per BARC's 2026 survey, 42% of Fabric buyers cited an existing Microsoft relationship as a material factor in selection, versus a 26% peer average.
positioningEvolution: Power BI as a standalone BI tool
Azure Synapse Analytics as an enterprise data warehouse
Microsoft Fabric: unified SaaS analytics platform (current)
customers: Broad existing Microsoft/Power BI installed base (specific Fabric-only references not independently confirmed)
sections.verticals: All major verticals given Microsoft's enterprise breadth - financial services, healthcare, retail, manufacturing and public sector all show up heavily in Fabric adoption data.
sections.partners: SAS itself is a notable partner - SAS Decision Builder is generally available on Fabric and listed on Microsoft Marketplace. Fabric also connects to Databricks (via OneLake shortcuts), Snowflake and BigQuery through mirroring.

top.point: Microsoft is simultaneously SAS's biggest platform partner and a growing analytics competitor.
subs: SAS Decision Builder runs natively on Fabric and SAS is a Gold FABCON sponsor, even as Fabric/Power BI competes directly for BI and analytics budget.

top.point: Fabric's pitch is bundled simplicity, not best-of-breed depth.
subs: It consolidates Synapse, Data Factory, Power BI and ADLS into one capacity-based SaaS subscription.

top.point: Capacity-based (CU) pricing is hard to forecast.
subs: Reviewers consistently flag throttling risk when usage exceeds the purchased tier, plus real FinOps/governance overhead to manage it well.

top.point: 42% of Fabric buyers cite existing Microsoft incumbency as a material selection factor (BARC).
subs: Often a default decision rather than a best-fit evaluation - a real opening in a competitive review.

top.point: Maturity gap: 37% of BARC-surveyed customers cite lack of internal know-how as a blocker to full value realization.
subs: Independent reviewers still describe the platform as "evolving," with governance/administration complexity increasing as adoption scales past a single team.

offerings.overview: Fabric is a fully managed SaaS platform unifying data engineering, warehousing, real-time analytics, data science and BI under one capacity model and one governance layer (OneLake + Purview).

items.name: Microsoft Fabric / OneLake
items.desc: Unified logical data lake with one capacity and one security model across all workloads.

items.name: Power BI / Direct Lake mode
items.desc: Near-real-time BI without import or DirectQuery performance tradeoffs.

items.name: Data Factory / Dataflow Gen2
items.desc: No-code ETL pipelines and orchestration.

items.name: Fabric Data Warehouse
items.desc: Compute/storage separated, SQL on open Delta/Parquet formats.

items.name: Real-Time Intelligence
items.desc: Event and streaming analytics with a Real-Time hub of no-code connectors.

items.name: Copilot in Fabric
items.desc: AI assistant available across all paid SKUs (F2 and above).

items.name: Fabric Data Agent
items.desc: Natural-language access to governed data, surfaced through Copilot Studio.

items.name: OneLake Catalog / Microsoft Purview
items.desc: Centralized governance, discovery and compliance across workspaces.

theirView: Positions Fabric as a single SaaS platform eliminating "tool chaos" across data engineering, warehousing, real-time analytics and BI.
Claims capacity-based pricing is simpler and more cost-effective than stitching together separate Power BI Premium, Synapse and Data Factory licenses.
Claims Copilot and Fabric Data Agent make advanced analytics accessible to non-technical business users through natural language.
sasView: Needs input - internal SAS positioning not yet available for this competitor.

productRebuttal.product: Needs input - specify which SAS product line to compare here.
sasView: Needs input - internal SAS positioning not yet available for this competitor.

dimensions.name: Native Microsoft 365 / Teams / Power Platform integration
Pricing simplicity for existing Microsoft shops

strengths: Enormous installed base and default incumbency in Microsoft-centric enterprises.
Genuinely unified architecture (OneLake) removing data duplication across workloads.
Fast-improving Copilot/agent capabilities across the whole suite.
Capacity model can be cheaper at scale for teams already paying for multiple separate Microsoft data products.
weaknesses: Capacity-based (CU) pricing is hard to forecast and can throttle performance without warning.
Governance/administration complexity increases sharply past a single workspace.
BARC-reported skills gap: 37% cite lack of internal know-how as a value-realization blocker.
Platform still described by independent reviewers as "evolving," with maturing best practices.

questions.a: Discovery questions and trap-setting answers require internal SAS positioning - add once available.

analystNote.text: Add current analyst recognition (Gartner Magic Quadrant, Forrester Wave, IDC MarketScape) relevant to Microsoft Fabric/Power BI once available.

attack: Ask what happens to their reporting/pipelines when they exceed purchased capacity - throttling is a documented pain point, and "buy a bigger SKU" is Microsoft's default answer.
Ask who owns FinOps/capacity planning for Fabric today - BARC data shows most customers underinvest here and get surprised by cost or performance.
Probe whether Fabric was chosen through a genuine best-fit evaluation or because "we're already a Microsoft shop" - incumbency bias is explicit in Microsoft's own buyer data.
Ask how they handle governed, auditable decisioning, not just reporting - Fabric's story is BI and data engineering, not the decision layer.
defend: "SAS is a separate platform we'd have to integrate" - SAS Decision Builder already runs natively on Microsoft Fabric and is available on Microsoft Marketplace; this is additive to a Fabric investment, not a rip-and-replace.

"Microsoft already does AI with Copilot" - Copilot is a general-purpose assistant layered on Fabric; SAS provides purpose-built, auditable decisioning and model risk management for regulated, high-stakes processes a general assistant isn't built to govern.
"It's cheaper since we already pay for Microsoft" - total cost isn't just license price; factor in FinOps overhead, governance tooling, and the risk of throttling on undersized capacity tiers.

sections.reframe: Don't compete with Fabric on being "the Microsoft-native platform" - that's an unwinnable, incumbency-driven argument in Microsoft shops. Reframe around what happens after the dashboard: whether the organization can turn an insight into a governed, auditable, production decision. Position SAS as complementary infrastructure that plugs directly into Fabric (via Decision Builder) rather than competing head-on for the BI budget line.
sections.betterTogether: Given SAS's own strategic partnership with Microsoft (Decision Builder GA on Fabric, Gold sponsorship at FABCON), most competitive situations here are better framed as "SAS plus Fabric" rather than "SAS versus Fabric" - position SAS as the decisioning and governance layer on top of a customer's existing Fabric investment rather than pitching a swap-out.

trustworthyAI.theirs: Microsoft leans on Purview-backed governance and Responsible AI tooling embedded across Copilot and Fabric, with sensitivity labels and compliance controls enforced through OneLake - a strong data-governance story, less specific on model-level decision auditability.
trustworthyAI.ours: SAS's approach embeds fairness and oversight at every step of the AI and analytics journey: robust data governance, transparent AI, and secure ModelOps processes.

competitors.name: Snowflake
competitors.category: Cloud data platform / AI Data Cloud
competitors.curator: Built from public research (Snowflake Q4 FY2026 earnings coverage, Cortex AI product documentation, G2/Capterra reviews). No internal Klue export available yet - Product Claims rebuttal, discovery questions and analyst-recognition fields are flagged needs input.

changelog.note: Initial card built from public research.

sections.background: Snowflake (NYSE: SNOW) reported Q4 FY2026 revenue of $1.3B (+30% YoY), product revenue of $1.2B (+30% YoY), and remaining performance obligations of $9.8B (+42% YoY), with an 11% non-GAAP operating margin and 61% adjusted free-cash-flow margin. More than 9,100 customer accounts now use Snowflake's AI capabilities, and Snowflake Intelligence scaled to 2,500+ accounts within three months of launch. The company has broadened well beyond its cloud-data-warehouse roots into a full "AI Data Cloud," adding Snowflake Postgres (via the Crunchy Data acquisition) for transactional workloads and acquiring Observe for observability/IT operations in FY2026.
sections.marketStrategy: Snowflake's strategy has evolved from "cloud data warehouse" to "AI Data Cloud" - bringing AI and LLMs to governed data rather than moving data out to external AI services, monetized through a new AI-credit billing layer alongside standard compute/storage credits.
positioningEvolution: Cloud data warehouse (SQL-native, separates storage/compute)
Data Cloud / data-sharing marketplace
AI Data Cloud with Cortex AI, agents and Snowflake Intelligence (current)
customers: 9,100+ accounts using Snowflake AI capabilities broadly across industries
sections.verticals: Advertising & media, financial services, healthcare & life sciences, manufacturing, public sector, retail & consumer goods, telecom, technology.
sections.partners: Multi-cloud across AWS, Azure and GCP; model partnerships spanning OpenAI, Anthropic, Meta (Llama) and Mistral; Cortex Code integrates with dbt, Apache Airflow, and external systems including Databricks and AWS Glue.

top.point: Snowflake is repositioning hard from "data warehouse" to "AI Data Cloud."
subs: Cortex AI, Snowflake Intelligence and Cortex Code are now central to its pitch, with 9,100+ accounts already using its AI capabilities.

top.point: Revenue and remaining performance obligations are both growing fast.
subs: $1.3B quarterly revenue (+30% YoY) and $9.8B in RPO (+42% YoY) signal long-term platform commitments, not just point deals.

top.point: Cost predictability is Snowflake's own most-cited user complaint.
subs: The same complaint SAS reps hear about nearly every consumption-priced cloud analytics platform, including Databricks.

top.point: Snowflake is explicitly expanding beyond analytics into adjacent categories.
subs: Postgres via Crunchy Data, observability via Observe - the platform-land-grab dynamic makes every renewal an opportunity for Snowflake to expand scope.

top.point: New AI credits (April 2026) are a separate billing currency on top of standard compute/storage.
subs: This adds a second axis of cost unpredictability that's easy to raise in a cost-conscious deal.

offerings.overview: Snowflake's core is a fully managed, multi-cloud data warehouse with storage, compute and services layered independently; Cortex AI extends that same governed perimeter to LLMs, search and agentic workflows.

items.name: Snowflake core platform
items.desc: Storage/compute/services layered architecture, pay-as-you-go across AWS, Azure, GCP.

items.name: Cortex AI
items.desc: AISQL functions, Cortex Search, Cortex Analyst (NL-to-SQL), Cortex Fine-tuning.

items.name: Cortex Agents / Snowflake Intelligence
items.desc: Multi-step agentic workflows and natural-language analytics across thousands of documents.

items.name: Cortex Code
items.desc: AI coding agent for the data stack; VS Code and Claude Code plugin support.

items.name: Snowflake Postgres
items.desc: Transactional workloads via the Crunchy Data acquisition.

items.name: Observe
items.desc: Observability and IT operations telemetry ingestion, acquired FY2026.

items.name: Snowpark
items.desc: Custom code execution (Python, Java, Scala) inside Snowflake.

items.name: Cortex Guard
items.desc: Safety/guardrails layer for AI applications built on Cortex.

theirView: Positions itself as the "control plane for the Agentic Enterprise," unifying data, tools and AI agents.
Claims bringing AI to governed data - rather than moving data to external AI services - is inherently safer and simpler than the alternative.
Claims a frictionless SQL experience and stronger governance maturity than code-first platforms like Databricks specifically.

Healthy free-cash-flow profile (61% margin) funding continued expansion.
Broad multi-model access (OpenAI, Anthropic, Meta, Mistral) inside one governed perimeter.

changelog.note: Initial card built from public research.

sections.background: Alteryx went private in March 2024 in a $4.4B take-private by Clearlake Capital and Insight Partners, ending its run as a public company amid slowing growth (down from 75% YoY in Q4 2022 to under 8% by Q3 2023). Prior to the buyout, 2023 revenue was roughly $970M with a GAAP net loss of $179M and sales & marketing spend around 60% of revenue - a growth profile public markets had stopped rewarding. Under Clearlake/Insight ownership, Alteryx has pushed hard into "Alteryx One," its unified AI-guided analytics platform, reporting it surpassed $1B in ARR in March 2026 alongside 380M+ automated workflows annually and an expanded Google Cloud partnership.
sections.marketStrategy: Historically a desktop-first, drag-and-drop data-prep tool for individual analysts, Alteryx's private-ownership strategy has shifted to "Alteryx One" - a unified, AI-guided platform spanning prep, automation and analytics, marketed at enterprise scale with a refreshed brand identity introduced in March 2026.
positioningEvolution: Desktop drag-and-drop data prep tool
Cloud-native analytics automation (post-Trifacta)

changelog.note: Initial card built from public research.

sections.background: Qlik was taken private by Thoma Bravo in a $3B deal in 2016. It acquired Talend in 2023 (itself a former Thoma Bravo portfolio company, originally bought for $2.4B), merging BI/analytics with data integration, quality and governance into one portfolio. In November 2024, Qlik closed a significant minority investment from ADIA (Abu Dhabi Investment Authority) alongside continued Thoma Bravo ownership, underscoring investor confidence in the combined analytics-plus-data-integration strategy. Qlik serves 40,000+ customers and has historically been named a Gartner Magic Quadrant Leader for BI and Analytics Platforms for 12+ consecutive years.
sections.marketStrategy: Qlik's long-standing differentiator is its "associative engine," letting users explore data relationships freely rather than through pre-defined query paths. Post-Talend, the strategy expanded to "end-to-end data integration, quality, analytics and AI," aiming to own the full pipeline from raw data to governed insight, not just the visualization layer.
positioningEvolution: QlikView: associative BI engine (1993 onward)
Qlik Sense: modern self-service analytics (2014)
End-to-end data integration + analytics platform (post-Talend, 2023-present)
sections.verticals: Financial services (risk/compliance/fraud detection), healthcare (patient/clinical outcomes), manufacturing, telecom (network/customer analytics), retail/logistics.
sections.partners: Cloud-neutral across AWS, Azure and GCP; broad SI/reseller network built around Talend's prior enterprise integration relationships.

top.point: Qlik's associative engine genuinely does something most query-based BI tools don't.
subs: It lets analysts explore any relationship in the data without pre-built drill paths - but it comes at real cost: pricing runs 10-20x Power BI's per-user cost at typical scale.

top.point: The Talend acquisition (2023) turned Qlik into an end-to-end data integration + analytics + governance vendor.
subs: This directly overlaps with parts of what SAS Viya plus SAS data management already do - now a broader platform conversation, not just a BI tool comparison.

top.point: Qlik has been private under Thoma Bravo since 2016, with a fresh ADIA minority investment in November 2024.
subs: Financially well-capitalized, but private ownership means less visibility into standalone financial performance.

top.point: In-memory architecture makes Qlik extremely fast for interactive exploration.
subs: It requires proportionally large server memory (roughly 150-200GB RAM per 500GB dataset on-prem) - a real infrastructure cost buyers often underestimate.

top.point: Migrating from the legacy QlikView product to Qlik Sense is reportedly painful.
subs: Apps often need significant rework - worth probing if the prospect is still running QlikView.

offerings.overview: Qlik's platform spans associative self-service BI, GenAI-assisted analytics, explainable predictive AI, and (post-Talend) full data integration, quality and governance.

sections.background: Tableau operates as a business unit of Salesforce (acquired in 2019 for roughly $15.7B) and has spent the last two years pivoting toward "Tableau Next," described as the first agentic analytics platform, built on Salesforce's Agentforce/Hyperforce architecture with Data 360 as its unified data layer. Tableau Next reached general availability progressively through 2025-2026 and became available to ISVs for embedding in April 2026. Pricing shifted in 2025 from consumption-based (Flex credits) to role-based licensing specifically to make budgeting more predictable.
sections.marketStrategy: Tableau's strategy has shifted from "best-in-class standalone visualization tool" to "the analytics intelligence layer for any AI agent in the Salesforce ecosystem." Tableau Next is positioned not as a product upgrade but as a full architectural reimagining for the agentic era, tightly coupled to Agentforce and Data 360.

Salesforce-owned BI platform, pivot to Tableau Cloud

customers: Staples (agentic seller experience built in 5 weeks with Tableau Next + Agentforce)
sections.verticals: All major verticals given Salesforce's own enterprise reach, especially sales-, service- and marketing-heavy organizations given the deep CRM tie-in.
sections.partners: Native integration with Agentforce, Slack, and Salesforce's broader Data 360 ecosystem; connects to external warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Databricks) without necessarily duplicating data.

top.point: Tableau Next is a genuine architectural bet, not just a rebrand.
subs: Built on Salesforce's Agentforce/Hyperforce stack with Data 360 as the unified semantic layer, positioned as the analytics layer for any AI agent, not just Tableau's own front end.

top.point: Classic Tableau (Cloud/Server) remains the product most prospects have actually used.
subs: Reviewers consistently flag performance degradation on very large datasets/live connections and a steep learning curve for calculated fields and LOD expressions.

top.point: Licensing cost is a persistent, top-cited complaint.
subs: Creator licenses run around $75/user/month; small/mid-market buyers frequently cite switching to or considering Power BI for cost reasons.

top.point: Tableau Next pricing moved to role-based licensing in 2025 specifically to fix consumption-cost unpredictability.
subs: Worth confirming whether a prospect evaluating Next has been quoted role-based pricing or is still comparing against legacy Flex-credit assumptions.

top.point: Some GenAI features pull from replicated Data Cloud copies rather than the live source of truth.
subs: Meaning "AI insights" can be generated from potentially stale data unless the architecture is set up carefully.

offerings.overview: Tableau spans classic visual analytics (Cloud/Server) and Tableau Next, a ground-up agentic analytics platform built on Salesforce's Agentforce and Data 360 infrastructure.

sections.background: IBM reported Q1 2026 revenue growth of 6% at constant currency, with diluted operating EPS up 19% year-over-year; full-year 2026 guidance commits to more than 5% constant-currency revenue growth and $1B of incremental free cash flow. IBM's AI strategy centers on watsonx (watsonx.ai for model building, watsonx.governance for explainability/compliance, watsonx.data as a governed lakehouse), reinforced by the Q1 2026 acquisition of Confluent for real-time, governed data streaming to models and agents. IBM has also shipped "AI editions" of legacy software including Db2, Cognos and MQ, embedding agentic capabilities directly into decades-old enterprise products, and continues to tie AI monetization to its mainframe business (z17 MIPS growth cited as accelerated by watsonx Code Assistant for Z usage).
sections.marketStrategy: IBM positions itself as the enterprise-safe, hybrid-cloud, regulatory-aligned AI vendor - "glass box" governance and explainability (watsonx.governance) as the wedge against both hyperscaler AI stacks and more permissive, open-source-first competitors like Databricks. Reference deployments span financial services (JPMorgan, BNY Mellon), healthcare (Mayo Clinic) and telco (Vodafone, T-Mobile).

IBM Business Analytics / Cloud Pak for Data (BI + governance suite)

sections.verticals: Financial services, healthcare/life sciences, telecom, government/public sector, manufacturing - IBM's traditional enterprise strongholds.
sections.partners: Deep hybrid-cloud partnerships via Red Hat/OpenShift; multi-model support inside watsonx spanning IBM's own Granite models alongside third-party LLMs.

top.point: IBM's central pitch is "glass box" trustworthy AI.

top.point: IBM just closed the Confluent acquisition (Q1 2026) to stream governed, real-time data to models and agents.
subs: A direct architectural answer to the same "AI needs live, governed data" problem SAS, Databricks and Snowflake are all solving differently.

top.point: Cognos Analytics consistently draws complexity and performance complaints.
subs: G2/Gartner reviewers repeatedly cite a steep learning curve and slow performance on large datasets/complex reports, a long-standing, still-unresolved pattern.

top.point: IBM is embedding agentic AI directly into legacy, decades-old products (Db2, Cognos, MQ "AI editions").
subs: A hybrid-modernization narrative aimed squarely at large, long-tenured enterprise accounts - like many SAS customers - that don't want to rip out existing infrastructure.

top.point: Across watsonx.ai, watsonx.data and Cognos, the consistent independent-review theme is the same.
subs: Powerful and enterprise-grade, but complex, expensive, and demanding of specialized skills to configure and operate well.

changelog.note: Initial card built from public research.

sections.background: TIBCO was founded in 1997 and taken private by Vista Equity Partners for roughly $4.2-4.3B in December 2014. In 2022, Vista and Elliott Investment Management's Evergreen Coast Capital merged TIBCO with the newly acquired Citrix Systems ($16.5B deal) to form Cloud Software Group, serving a combined 400,000+ customers and 98% of the Fortune 500. TIBCO's analytics business (built around Spotfire, acquired in 2007, and Information Builders/IBI, acquired 2021) now operates as a business unit within this larger, heavily leveraged private-equity structure rather than as an independently focused analytics company.
sections.marketStrategy: TIBCO built its analytics position primarily through acquisition (30+ deals historically) rather than organic product development, with Spotfire as its flagship visual/predictive analytics tool and ModelOps added to address data-science-at-scale deployment. Since the Citrix merger, TIBCO operates inside a much larger, debt-financed digital-workspace-plus-data conglomerate whose strategic priorities aren't solely about analytics.
positioningEvolution: Real-time messaging / integration middleware ("The Information Bus")
Analytics roll-up via acquisition (Spotfire, StreamBase, IBI)
Business unit within Cloud Software Group, post-Citrix merger (current)
sections.verticals: Manufacturing, life sciences, energy, financial services, telecom - TIBCO's original real-time integration/messaging heritage skews toward operational and IoT-heavy verticals.
sections.partners: Historically broad system-integrator network; now operates within Cloud Software Group's combined Citrix/TIBCO partner ecosystem.

top.point: TIBCO's future is now tied to Cloud Software Group, the heavily leveraged 2022 merger of TIBCO and Citrix under Vista/Elliott.
subs: The merger required billions in acquisition debt, and analysts at the time questioned whether TIBCO and Citrix have any obvious technology synergy.

top.point: TIBCO's own analysts have historically acknowledged that integrating its acquisitions took years.
subs: Spotfire in particular took years to find its place in the portfolio - the same integration challenge likely applies to any deeper Citrix/TIBCO product work going forward.

competitors.curator: Built from public research (Sacra financial analysis, G2/Gartner reviews, CheckThat.ai review synthesis). No internal Klue export available yet - Product Claims rebuttal, discovery questions and analyst-recognition fields are flagged needs input.

changelog.note: Initial card built from public research.

sections.background: DataRobot has raised roughly $1.05-1.3B across 12 funding rounds, reaching a $6.3B valuation at its July 2021 Series G. That valuation has not held: third-party fund marks from Franklin Templeton and BlackRock in late 2025/early 2026 imply the company is now worth only a small fraction of that peak - BlackRock's Series F position was marked at roughly 28% of its original cost basis. No further financing valuation has been publicly announced since 2021, and the last disclosed revenue figure was over $100M ARR with "high double-digit growth" as of November 2020 - meaning there is no current, independently confirmed revenue or valuation figure available. The company has pivoted its positioning to an "Agent Workforce Platform" for agentic AI, and was again named a Leader in Gartner's Magic Quadrant for the "Agentic Era."
sections.marketStrategy: DataRobot's original wedge was AutoML - automating comparison of dozens of models in parallel with built-in explainability (SHAP, bias detection). Its current strategy pivots that automation heritage toward "agentic AI," an Agent Workforce Platform aimed at moving agents from demo to governed production use, targeting the same regulated, high-stakes enterprise buyers (financial services, insurance, government) that SAS also targets.
positioningEvolution: Automated machine learning (AutoML) for predictive models
MLOps / model governance and monitoring (via Algorithmia)
Agent Workforce Platform for agentic AI (current)
customers: Historically roughly a third of the Fortune 50 (specific current named references not independently confirmed)
sections.verticals: Financial services, insurance, manufacturing, supply chain, federal government - DataRobot has leaned into FedRAMP/NIST compliance as a differentiator for regulated and public-sector buyers specifically.
sections.partners: Deployment partnership with Dell for federal go-to-market; competes directly against, but also sits adjacent to, hyperscaler ML stacks (AWS SageMaker, Google Vertex AI, Azure AI Foundry).

top.point: DataRobot's valuation has fallen sharply from its 2021 peak.
subs: Independent fund marks from Franklin Templeton and BlackRock in late 2025/early 2026 imply the company is worth a small fraction of its $6.3B Series G valuation - fair, publicly documented context for a vendor-viability conversation.

top.point: No new financing valuation or updated revenue figure has been made public since 2021 (valuation) and late 2020 (revenue).
subs: DataRobot's current financial health is genuinely difficult for a prospect to verify independently.

top.point: DataRobot faces direct platform-consolidation pressure from AWS SageMaker, Google Vertex AI and Azure AI Foundry.
subs: All of which bundle comparable MLOps/AI-governance tooling into infrastructure enterprises are already paying for - a real risk to standalone-platform procurement.

top.point: Pricing is opaque and sold in bulk increments (workers/production keys bundled rather than sold individually).
subs: Reviewers specifically flag this as inflexible and among their "most expensive vendor partnerships."

top.point: DataRobot's own "Unmet AI Needs 2026" research found 94% of teams see unexpected costs after deploying agentic AI.
subs: Averaging 7.3 months from idea to production - a data point DataRobot uses to sell its platform, but one reviewer feedback suggests applies to DataRobot deployments themselves too.

attack: Ask directly about DataRobot's most recent funding round, valuation, and revenue disclosures - the last public revenue figure is from 2020, and third-party fund marks suggest the 2021 valuation has fallen sharply since; this is fair, publicly documented territory for a vendor-viability conversation.

competitors.name: Dataiku
competitors.curator: Built from public research (Dataiku LLM Mesh documentation, Danelfin/analyst coverage, G2/Gartner reviews). No internal Klue export available yet - Product Claims rebuttal, discovery questions and analyst-recognition fields are flagged needs input.

changelog.note: Initial card built from public research.

sections.background: Founded in Paris in 2013 and now headquartered in New York, Dataiku raised a Series F in 2022 valuing the company at $3.7B (backed by Iconiq Capital, CapitalG, Tiger Global, and Snowflake Ventures) and reportedly crossed $200M in ARR by 2023. Dataiku has been named a Leader in Gartner's Magic Quadrant for AI Platforms for Data Science and ML for five consecutive years through 2026, and has expanded aggressively into generative AI and agentic governance via its "LLM Mesh" architecture, which centralizes multi-LLM access, routing, cost control and governance for enterprise GenAI applications.
sections.marketStrategy: Dataiku's core pitch is "Everyday AI" - a single platform where both technical data scientists and non-technical business experts collaborate on the same projects via no-code, low-code and full-code interfaces. The LLM Mesh extends this to generative AI, positioning Dataiku as a vendor-neutral governance layer across any LLM provider or cloud, explicitly to avoid the kind of lock-in enterprises experienced with multi-cloud infrastructure.
positioningEvolution: Collaborative data science platform for technical + non-technical users (2013 onward)
End-to-end MLOps and AI governance suite
LLM Mesh: multi-model generative AI and agent governance layer (current)
sections.verticals: Financial services, pharma/life sciences, manufacturing, retail, energy, public sector.
sections.partners: Explicitly vendor-neutral across clouds and data platforms - integrates with Snowflake, Databricks, AWS, Azure, Google Cloud; LLM Mesh connects to 15+ LLM providers including AWS, Google Cloud, Microsoft and Databricks-hosted models.

top.point: Dataiku's core differentiator is genuinely collaborative.
subs: The same platform serves data scientists (full-code Python/R/SQL) and business analysts (no-code/low-code) on the same projects - a direct challenge to SAS's own "every persona" positioning, on very similar ground.

top.point: The LLM Mesh is a real architectural bet against LLM/vendor lock-in.
subs: A governed, model-agnostic gateway for routing, cost control and safety across 15+ LLM providers - positioning Dataiku as infrastructure-neutral in a way Databricks and hyperscaler-native tools aren't.

top.point: Dataiku has been a Gartner Magic Quadrant Leader for five consecutive years through 2026.
subs: A durable, credible analyst position that will come up in almost any competitive evaluation.

top.point: Structural risk flagged by analysts: hyperscalers keep closing feature gaps with bundled, subsidized tooling.
subs: As AWS SageMaker, Azure ML and Google Vertex AI narrow the gap, Dataiku's standalone value proposition narrows toward workflow/UX rather than unique capability.

top.point: Performance and cost are the most consistent independent-review complaints.
subs: Reviewers cite slow performance on very large datasets/complex workflows and pricing that's difficult to justify for smaller teams or simpler use cases.

Real architectural differentiation via LLM Mesh's vendor-neutral governance.
Strong reference customer base across regulated and complex industries (pharma, banking, manufacturing).

questions.a: Discovery questions and trap-setting answers require internal SAS positioning - add once available.

analystNote.text: Add current analyst recognition (Gartner Magic Quadrant, Forrester Wave, IDC MarketScape) relevant to Dataiku once available.

attack: Ask about performance at their actual data scale - slow performance with large datasets/complex workflows is one of the most consistently repeated complaints across review platforms.
Ask how they're budgeting for Dataiku given that pricing is frequently cited as hard to justify below a certain team size or use-case complexity.
Ask how Dataiku's LLM Mesh governance compares to a purpose-built, regulated decisioning platform when the output is a high-stakes business decision, not just a chat response.
defend: "Dataiku already bridges technical and business users" - SAS Viya offers the same blended-persona experience (GUI plus open-language support) while carrying that work through statistically rigorous, auditable decisioning that Dataiku's LLM Mesh governance doesn't specifically target.

"Dataiku is vendor-neutral, SAS isn't" - SAS Viya itself integrates across Snowflake, Databricks and Microsoft Fabric; vendor-neutrality isn't unique to Dataiku, and SAS pairs it with a longer regulated-decisioning track record.

sections.reframe: Don't compete with Dataiku on collaborative UX or multi-cloud/multi-LLM neutrality - both are genuine, credible strengths reinforced by five straight years as a Gartner Leader. Reframe around the decision layer specifically: LLM Mesh governs model/agent access and cost; SAS governs the actual regulated business decision those agents and models feed into, a narrower and more specialized bar Dataiku's governance story doesn't directly target.
sections.betterTogether: In accounts where Dataiku is already the data-science collaboration hub, position SAS as the decisioning and model-risk-management layer for the subset of use cases that are regulated or high-stakes enough to need it, rather than displacing Dataiku's broader data science workflow.

trustworthyAI.theirs: Dataiku's trust story centers on the LLM Mesh's governance layer - policy enforcement, usage limits, spend monitoring and audit trails at the LLM connection layer - plus Agent Guard Services for evaluating agent reliability and business alignment, a genuinely modern and credible architecture, though focused on AI/agent governance rather than statistical model risk management specifically.
trustworthyAI.ours: SAS's approach embeds fairness and oversight at every step of the AI and analytics journey: robust data governance, transparent AI, and secure ModelOps processes.
